request.question: Who wrote the play "...but the clouds..." starring Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup?

title: Leo the Last
Make Mine Mink
Charlie Bubbles
Rockaby
Rough for Radio II
... but the clouds ...
Hotel Paradiso (film)
Footfalls
Ghost Trio (play)
Ronald Pickup
sentences: Leo the Last is a 1970 British drama film directed by John Boorman, based on the play "The Prince" by George Tabori, starring Marcello Mastroianni and Billie Whitelaw.
Make Mine Mink is a 1960 British comedy farce film directed by Robert Asher and featuring Terry-Thomas, Athene Seyler, Hattie Jacques and Billie Whitelaw.
The screenplay concerns a group of eccentric misfits who go on a spree, stealing mink coats for charity in a Robin Hood-style gang.
It was based on the play "Breath of Spring" by Peter Coke, and its sequels.
Seyer and Elspeth Duxbury reprised their stage roles from the London production of "Breath of Spring".
Charlie Bubbles is a 1967 British comedy-drama film starring Billie Whitelaw and Albert Finney, and also featuring a young Liza Minnelli.
It was listed to compete at the 1968 Cannes Film Festival, but the festival was cancelled due to the events of May 1968 in France.
Rockaby is a short one-woman play by Samuel Beckett.

It was written in English in 1980, at the request of Daniel Labeille, who produced it on behalf of "Programs in the Arts", State University of New York, for a festival and symposium in commemoration of Beckett's 75th birthday.
The play premiered on April 8, 1981 at the State University of New York at Buffalo, starring Billie Whitelaw and directed by Alan Schneider.
A documentary film, "Rockaby", by D. A. Pennebaker and Chris Hegedus records the rehearsal process and the first performance.
This production went on to be performed at the Annex at La MaMa Experimental Theatre Club, and, in December 1982, at the Cottesloe, Royal National Theatre, London.
Rough for Radio II is a radio play by Samuel Beckett.
It was written in French in 1961 as Pochade radiophonique and published in "Minuit" 16, November 1975.
Beckett translated the work into English shortly before its broadcast on BBC Radio 3 on 13 April 1976.
Martin Esslin directed Harold Pinter (Animator), Billie Whitelaw (Stenographer) and Patrick Magee (Fox).
The English-language version was first published in "Ends and Odds" (Grove, 1976, Faber, 1977) as Radio II.
... but the clouds ... is a television play by Samuel Beckett.
Beckett wrote it between October–November 1976 “to replace a film of "Play" which the BBC had sent [him] for approval (and which he had rejected)” due to “the poor quality of the film”.
Donald McWhinnie directed Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup.
It was first broadcast on 17 April 1977 as part of a programme of three Beckett plays entitled ‘Shades’ on BBC2.
It was first published in "Ends and Odds" (Faber) 1977.
An early title for the piece was Poetry only love.
Hotel Paradiso is a Metrocolor 1966 film released by Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer in Panavision.
It was directed by Peter Glenville and based on the play "L'Hôtel du libre échange" by Maurice Desvallières and Georges Feydeau.
The film allowed Alec Guinness to reprise the role he had played in the London West End theatre production of Hotel Paradiso, which opened at the Winter Garden Theatre, Drury Lane, London.
on 2 May 1956.
Guinness played alongside Martita Hunt (Angelique), Irene Worth (Marcelle), Frank Pettingell (Cot), Kenneth Williams (Maxime) and Billie Whitelaw (Victoire).
Douglas Byng also reprised his part from the stage play.
Footfalls is a play by Samuel Beckett.
It was written in English, between 2 March and December 1975 and was first performed at the Royal Court Theatre as part of the Samuel Beckett Festival, on May 20, 1976 directed by Beckett himself.
Billie Whitelaw, for whom the piece had been written, played May whilst Rose Hill voiced the mother.
Ghost Trio is a television play, written in English by Samuel Beckett.
It was written in 1975, taped in October 1976 and the first broadcast was on BBC2 on 17 April 1977 as part of the "Lively Arts" programme Beckett himself entitled "Shades".
Donald McWhinnie directed (supervised by Beckett) with Ronald Pickup and Billie Whitelaw.
The play’s original title was to be Tryst.
"On Beckett’s notebook, the word was crossed out vigorously and the new title "Ghost Trio" written next to it.
On the title page of the BBC script the same handwritten title change can be found, indicating that it must have been corrected at the very last minute."
Ronald Alfred Pickup (born 7 June 1940) is an English actor who has been active in television and film since 1964.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

hop1_docs: ... but the clouds ... | ... but the clouds ... is a television play by Samuel Beckett.  Beckett wrote it between October–November 1976 “to replace a film of "Play" which the BBC had sent [him] for approval (and which he had rejected)” due to “the poor quality of the film”.  Donald McWhinnie directed Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup.  It was first broadcast on 17 April 1977 as part of a programme of three Beckett plays entitled ‘Shades’ on BBC2.  It was first published in "Ends and Odds" (Faber) 1977.  An early title for the piece was Poetry only love.

Ghost Trio (play) | Ghost Trio is a television play, written in English by Samuel Beckett.  It was written in 1975, taped in October 1976 and the first broadcast was on BBC2 on 17 April 1977 as part of the "Lively Arts" programme Beckett himself entitled "Shades".  Donald McWhinnie directed (supervised by Beckett) with Ronald Pickup and Billie Whitelaw.  The play’s original title was to be Tryst.  "On Beckett’s notebook, the word was crossed out vigorously and the new title "Ghost Trio" written next to it.  On the title page of the BBC script the same handwritten title change can be found, indicating that it must have been corrected at the very last minute."
Leo the Last | Leo the Last is a 1970 British drama film directed by John Boorman, based on the play "The Prince" by George Tabori, starring Marcello Mastroianni and Billie Whitelaw.

Private Schulz | Private Schulz is a BBC television comedy drama serial set mostly in Germany, during and immediately after World War II.  It stars Michael Elphick in the title role and Ian Richardson playing various parts.  Other notable actors included Tony Caunter, Billie Whitelaw, Billy Murray and Mark Wingett.
Ronald Pickup | Ronald Alfred Pickup (born 7 June 1940) is an English actor who has been active in television and film since 1964.

Shadey | Shadey is a 1985 British comedy film directed by Philip Saville and starring Antony Sher, Billie Whitelaw and Patrick Macnee.  The screenplay concerns a man with clairvoyant qualities who is recruited by British intelligence for a secret mission.  The film grossed over $65,817.
Tangiers (1982 film) | Tangier is a 1982 American-Moroccan thriller film directed by Michael E. Briant and starring Ronny Cox, Billie Whitelaw and Glynis Barber.
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: the television play "... but the clouds ..." (and related Beckett television plays)

- "'... but the clouds ...' is a television play by Samuel Beckett." (passage [1])
- "Donald McWhinnie directed Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup." (passage [1])
- "first broadcast on 17 April 1977 as part of a programme of three Beckett plays entitled ‘Shades’ on BBC2." (passage [1])
- Related/overlapping item: "Ghost Trio is a television play... written in English by Samuel Beckett" and likewise notes "Donald McWhinnie directed ... with Ronald Pickup and Billie Whitelaw" and the same broadcast date 17 April 1977 (passage [2]) — possible title/entry overlap or confusion between the two plays.
- Ambiguity/missing fact: whether "Ghost Trio" and "... but the clouds ..." are distinct pieces or if there is any editorial/title conflation in listings (bridge entity: the Beckett TV plays broadcast 17 April 1977).
- Next-hop retrieval directives (priority):
  1. No additional retrieval needed — passages unambiguously attribute authorship to Samuel Beckett for "... but the clouds ...".
  2. (If verifying title distinctness) Fetch "Ghost Trio (play)" page — inspect lede/infobox and "Production/Broadcast" sections.
  3. (Optional) Fetch "... but the clouds ..." page or section (lede/infobox, "Production") for confirmation of title variants and publication history.
hop2_docs: Cloud collaboration | Cloud collaboration is a way of sharing and co-authoring computer files through the use of cloud computing, whereby documents are uploaded to a central "cloud" for storage, where they can then be accessed by others.

Cloud atlas | A cloud atlas is a pictorial key (or an atlas) to the nomenclature of clouds. Early cloud atlases were an important element in the training of meteorologists and in weather forecasting, and the author of a 1923 atlas stated that "increasing use of the air as a means of transportation will require and lead to a detailed knowledge of all the secrets of cloud building."
The Cloud Searchers | The Cloud Searchers is a fantasy graphic novel which was written in 2010.  It is the third volume in the Amulet series.  It was written and drawn by author Kazu Kibuishi.

St. Cloud Metropolitan Transit Commission | The St. Cloud Metropolitan Transit Commission, branded as Metro Bus, is the primary provider of mass transportation in St. Cloud, Minnesota.  Service is provided daily using a fleet of 37 full-sized buses.  The agency was formed in 1969 after the private St. Cloud Bus Lines began to cut routes and increase fares, leading the Minnesota State Legislature to establish a Transit Authority to make up for perceived inadequate service.

Donald Leka | Donald Leka is an entrepreneur in the information technology industry and pioneer in the cloud computing space.  Leka is the Founder, Chairman and CEO of Jumptuit (originally TransMedia), the creator of the award-winning cloud computing platform Glide OS and its successor, Jumptuit.  Leka is also co-author of "Cloud Computing: The Glide OS Story, Solving The Cross Platform Puzzle".

Clouds without Water | Clouds without Water is a poetry collection by Aleister Crowley (1875–1947), an English author, occult magician, mountaineer and founder of the religious philosophy of Thelema.  "Clouds without Water" was one of many of Crowley's eccentric works published in his lifetime and was first issued in 1909.  The title comes from a passage in Jude 1:13 which is quoted at the beginning of the book:

Albert Riggenbach | Albert Riggenbach was a 19th-century meteorologist and co-author of one of the first cloud atlases, the "International Cloud Atlas".  His doctoral dissertation ("Habilitationsschrift") concerned observations of the first described Bishop's Ring.
response.summary_2: "'... but the clouds ...' is a television play by Samuel Beckett." Donald McWhinnie directed Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup, and it was first broadcast on 17 April 1977 as part of the BBC2 programme "Shades".
